title: Research Activities
text: Our group specializes in the continuum mechanics of soft, living, and active materials. By bridging experimental characterization, advanced bioimaging, and state-of-the-art computational modeling, we tackle critical challenges in physiology, mechanobiology, and tissue engineering.

title: Research Activities
text:
Corneal Mechanics & Refractive Surgery
We investigate the intricate lamellar microstructure of the healthy and pathological cornea to predict its response to surgical interventions and degenerative diseases. Our multiscale approach combines advanced experimental testing (bulge tests, Optical Coherence Tomography, and 3D Digital Volume Correlation) with custom, microstructure-informed finite element models to characterize anisotropy, structural gradients, and active collagen remodeling.
Key topics: Corneal Anisotropy OCT & DVC Imaging Refractive Surgery Simulation
📚 View Relevant Publications

title: Research Activities
text:
Full Ocular Mechanics & Imaging
Beyond the cornea, we model the mechanical environment of the entire eye globe. This includes utilizing AI-based segmentation on MRI data to generate patient-specific meshes of the eye and extraocular muscles. By simulating muscular contraction, we characterize the influence of eye movements on long-term ocular growth, remodeling, and the epidemic progression of myopia during childhood, paving the way for medical optics interventions.
Key topics: Extraocular Muscles & Myopia AI MRI Segmentation Growth & Remodeling
📚 View Relevant Publications

title: Research Activities
text:
Mechanics of Blood Clots & Hemostasis
Understanding the mechanics of thrombus formation and dissolution is crucial for diagnosing and tackling cardiovascular diseases. By coupling solid mechanics with advanced optical tools (biophotonics), we characterize the 3D microstructural architecture and microrheology of venous blood clots. Our multiscale approach quantifies fibrin network hypercoagulability to predict clot stability and embolization risks under physiological conditions.
Key topics: Venous Microrheology Thrombus Visco-hyperelasticity Hypercoagulability
📚 View Relevant Publications

title: Research Activities
text:
Vascular Mechanobiology & Vessels-on-a-Chip
To recreate the native cellular microenvironment, we develop in vitro vascular platforms (vessels-on-a-chip) utilizing bespoke collagen hydrogels. These platforms allow us to study the patient-specific mechanobiology of human primary Smooth Muscle Cells (SMCs) and Endothelial Cells. By engineering dynamic cyclic perfusion systems, we mimic physiological mechanical loading and track mechanotransduction pathways—such as YAP/TAZ—that drive vascular aging, phenotypic switching, and aortic aneurysms.
Key topics: Vessels-on-a-chip Patient-Specific Mechanobiology Endothelial Crosstalk
📚 View Relevant Publications

title: Research Activities
text:
Magneto-Active Biomaterials & Cell Stimulation
To isolate the effect of 3D mechanical stress on cellular behavior, we engineer smart, magneto-responsive hydrogels. By embedding magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4) within collagen matrices, we can apply highly controlled, dynamic field gradients to stimulate resident cells. Coupled with multiphoton microscopy and Digital Volume Correlation, this novel platform allows us to precisely quantify how local mechanical forces dictate matrix remodeling and SMC contractility.
Key topics: Magneto-responsive Hydrogels Matrix Remodeling
📚 View Relevant Publications

title: Research Activities
section: Soft Matter Mechanics & Advanced Numerical Methods
text:
Soft Matter Mechanics & Advanced Numerical Methods
We push the boundaries of computational mechanics to decipher complex biological interactions. Our work includes multi-scale poroviscoelastic modeling of living tissues and hydrogels, capturing the dynamics of interstitial fluid and fibrillar architectures. By coupling 3D Digital Volume Correlation (DVC) on high-resolution OCT images with inverse problem frameworks, we successfully map local tissue deformations. This fuels our pioneering work in 3D Traction Force Microscopy (TFM), allowing us to measure the exact micromechanical stresses exerted by cells within their 3D native environment.
Key topics: 3D Traction Force Microscopy Poroviscoelasticity Inverse Problems
📚 View Relevant Publications

title: Parsa Namazian
text:
LinkedIn
  

      

    
    
  


Parsa Namazian is a Master’s intern conducting a lab rotation at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS). His research focuses on computational biomechanics, specifically modeling the complex mechanical behaviors of cells within tissue-engineered scaffolds.


Background
Parsa is currently pursuing his Master’s degree, building a strong analytical foundation in computational mechanics and biomechanical engineering. His academic training equips him with the advanced numerical and programming skills required to simulate the behavior of complex biological systems.



Internship Overview
Project: Numerical assessment of cell orientation impact on collagen hydrogels
During his lab rotation in May 2026, Parsa is investigating the mechanobiological principles governing how cells orient and reorient themselves within 3D collagen environments.
His core responsibilities and methodologies include:

Computational Modeling: Studying the mechanical effects of cell orientation and the dynamic processes of cellular reorientation within soft hydrogel matrices.
Numerical Tool Development: Utilizing and expanding the laboratory’s custom computational toolbox, which is built upon the dolfin_mech Python library.
Mechanobiology Simulation: Running finite element simulations to predict how local cellular alignment impacts the global mechanical properties and structural integrity of tissue scaffolds.

Through this rotation, he is gaining hands-on experience in advanced numerical methods and the computational modeling of active, living materials.
Key topics: Computational Biomechanics Cell Orientation Python / dolfin_mech







 Back to top

title: Giulia Merlini
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Personal Website
  
  
    
     PhD Thesis
  

      

    
    
  


Giulia Merlini is a former PhD student who successfully defended her thesis in 2025. Her research operates at the intersection of applied mathematics, computational mechanics, and materials science, focusing on the theoretical and numerical modeling of complex materials.


Background
During her doctoral studies, Giulia worked in a highly multidisciplinary environment, collaborating closely with leading applied mathematics and mechanics research groups. In addition to her work at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS), she was strongly affiliated with the MATHERIALS team at Inria and the CERMICS laboratory at École des Ponts ParisTech. This unique background provided her with a robust foundation in scientific computing, mathematical analysis, and the mechanics of materials.



PhD Overview & Expertise
Giulia’s doctoral research focused on developing advanced mathematical frameworks and numerical methods to simulate and understand material behavior. By leveraging rigorous mathematical tools, she tackled complex problems related to multiscale modeling and the structural properties of materials.
Her core technical expertise includes:

Mathematical Modeling: Developing and analyzing theoretical models (such as PDEs) to describe the complex, non-linear behavior of materials.
Numerical Simulation & Scientific Computing: Implementing robust numerical methods to solve advanced mechanical and physical problems.
Multiscale Methods: Bridging the gap between microscopic structural properties and macroscopic mechanical responses.
Applied Mathematics: Applying rigorous analytical techniques to ensure the stability, accuracy, and efficiency of computational models.

Key topics: Applied Mathematics Computational Mechanics Multiscale Modeling Scientific Computing







 Back to top

title: Qian Wu
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     PhD Thesis
  

      

    
    
  


Qian Wu is a former PhD student at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS), having successfully defended her thesis in 2025. She is a highly versatile researcher specializing in biomechanics and materials mechanics, with deep expertise in experimental characterization and AI-based image analysis.
She aims to actively contribute to scientific innovation, its industrial application, and the transfer and valorization of knowledge.


Background
Before completing her PhD, Qian gained valuable industrial experience in automotive engineering. Her work included the testing and validation of complex systems—notably the tuning of ESP calibration—and she participated in the development of electromechanical products, such as vacuum pumps for brake booster systems. This background provided her with a strong foundation in R&D project coordination, technical specification management, and multidisciplinary collaboration.



PhD Overview & Expertise
During her doctoral research, Qian investigated the structure–property relationships in complex materials. She developed custom algorithms and data processing pipelines using Python and MATLAB to extract quantitative insights from highly complex biological and material samples.
Her core technical expertise includes:

Experimental Mechanics: Developing experimental protocols and conducting multiscale characterization of soft and hard materials using Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT), Optical Microscopy, and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM).
Advanced Image Analysis: Computing deformation fields via Digital Image Correlation (DIC), extracting features via OCT segmentation, geometric reconstruction from point clouds, and preparing/annotating datasets for Machine Learning models (using ImageJ, Ilastik, and ParaView).
Modeling & Engineering Tools: Utilizing Python, MATLAB, R, COMSOL Multiphysics, and CATIA for automated data analysis and predictive modeling.

Key topics: Experimental Mechanics Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) Digital Image Correlation (DIC) Image Analysis & ML

title: Guillaume Patt Laffite
text:
ORCID
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Sainbiose Profile
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Guillaume Patt Laffite is a multidisciplinary researcher in Biomaterials and Biomedical Engineering, specializing in the design and characterization of 3D and 4D printed smart polymers. His work focuses on developing shape-morphing polymer materials, structural composites, and responsive scaffolds intended for advanced biomedical applications, such as bone fixation and tissue engineering.

He earned his Ph.D. in Materials Science and Engineering in 2024 from the École des Mines de Saint-Étienne. His doctoral research, which was part of the Horizon Europe BoneFix project, focused on the design of a 4D-printed, reinforced resin composite membrane intended as a novel bone fixation patch. His academic background also includes an M.Sc. in Biomedical Engineering from Paris University. Guillaume possesses extensive and versatile expertise bridging materials formulation (photo-curable resins), additive manufacturing (FDM), biomechanical testing, and in vitro biological assessments (such as macrophage cell culture for inflammatory response validation).

Prior to joining the laboratory, Guillaume completed a postdoctoral fellowship at the Centre for Biomedical and Healthcare Engineering (Sainbiose lab, Saint-Étienne), where he developed shape-morphing polymers and validated inflammatory responses using macrophage cultures.
More recently, he joined the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS) at École Polytechnique as a Postdoctoral Researcher, bringing his versatile skill set in smart materials, advanced characterization (Micro-CT, SEM, Confocal microscopy), and 3D printing to tackle new challenges in soft tissue biomechanics and mechanobiology.






 Back to top

title: Kateřina Škardová
text:
Orcid
  
  
      Researchgate
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  

      

    
    
  


Kateřina Škardová is a researcher in Biomechanics and Computational Modeling, specializing in the development of patient-specific biophysical models and advanced numerical methods. Her work focuses on characterizing the complex mechanics and hemodynamics of the cardiovascular system. She has direct expertise in solving partial differential equations, estimating material parameters from clinical imaging (such as MRI), and developing robust computational frameworks for biomedical applications.

She earned her Ph.D. from the Czech Technical University in Prague (Faculty of Nuclear Sciences and Physical Engineering). Her doctoral work allied theoretical developments, numerical assessments, and clinical application, successfully bridging mathematics and cardiology through collaborations with the Institute of Clinical and Experimental Medicine in Prague. Her research provided critical insights into left ventricular torsion and non-invasive parameter estimation, while emphasizing the coupling of medical data with biophysical models. This program involved strong international collaboration, notably with the Inria-UTSW Associated Team.

More recently, she is part of post-doctoral research projects at the Institut Polytechnique de Paris:

Post-Doctoral Researcher (08/2023–Present): A joint appointment at École Polytechnique (Solid Mechanics Laboratory - LMS) and Inria (M3DISIM team). Her current research is dedicated to inverse problems in large strains (such as the Equilibrium Gap Method) and hybrid approaches combining reduced-order modeling (Proper Generalised Decomposition) with deep learning to provide real-time solutions for parameterized mechanics problems.







 Back to top

title: Mariana Otoya Trevisani
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Mariana Otoya Trevisani is a PhD candidate in Biomechanics at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS). Her research focuses on the multiscale characterization of the extracellular matrix to better understand the biophysical mechanisms driving structural failures in the thoracic aorta.


Background
Mariana holds an engineering degree from IMT Mines Albi and a Master’s in Material and Structural Mechanics from the Université de Toulouse. She complemented her training with a second Master’s degree in Biomechanics and Biomedical Engineering from the Institut Polytechnique de Paris.
Throughout her academic journey, she has developed broad technical expertise, notably in designing mechanical test benches for medical technologies, studying collagen hydrogel microstructures, and developing innovative characterization methods. Her outstanding academic and project work was recently recognized when she was named a laureate of the Prix Fondation Mines-Télécom.



Thesis Overview
Title: Multi-scale mechanical characterization of extra-cellular matrix remodelling by aortic smooth muscle cells
Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm (TAA) involves a pathological “feedback loop” where Smooth Muscle Cells (SMCs) dysregulate the Extracellular Matrix (ECM). Mariana’s PhD project uses 3D collagen hydrogels to quantify how these cellular shifts drive structural tissue failure.
Her experimental pipeline includes:

Baseline Characterization: Assessing acellular hydrogels using macro-rheology, micro-indentation, and uniaxial traction to establish initial viscoelastic properties.
Simulating the Aneurysmal Environment: Seeding thoracic aortic SMCs into the matrices and subjecting them to mechanical (traction and compression) and chemical perturbations.
Anisotropic Scaffold Engineering: Mimicking the aortic media with pre-aligned collagen fibers to isolate how structural “guidance” dictates remodeling efficiency and directionality.
Post-Culture Analysis: Combining mechanical testing with high-resolution imaging (Confocal and Second Harmonic Generation) to correlate microstructural fiber density with emergent bulk stiffness.

By bridging local fiber architecture and global tissue mechanics, this research aims to provide a predictive framework for the biophysical transitions leading to catastrophic aortic wall failure.







 Back to top

title: Jean-Marc ALLAIN
text:
ORCID
  
  
    
     WebofScience
  
  
    
     Gitlab
  
  
    
     Ecole Polytechnique Profile
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Jean-Marc Allain is a faculty member (Associate Professor / Professeur chargé de cours) in the Department of Mechanics at École Polytechnique (Institut Polytechnique de Paris). He conducts his research at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS) and was part of the M3DISIM research team (a joint research group involving École Polytechnique, CNRS, and Inria). He defended his PhD in physics in 2005 at Université Paris 7, focusing on the “Instabilities of inhomogeneous lipid membranes: biological implications.”


Research Interests
Jean-Marc Allain’s research sits at the intersection of mechanics, biology, and physics. His work focuses primarily on biomechanics and multiscale experimental mechanobiology, with the overarching goal of linking the microstructure of biological tissues to their macroscopic mechanical properties.
His main areas of research include:

Skin Mechanics and Aging: Studying the mechanical behavior of the dermis and the organization of its collagen fibers (e.g., using multiphoton microscopy) to understand how aging impacts the skin.
Vascular Mechanics and Clot Microstructure: Analyzing the organization of the fibrin network and the rheology of blood clots to better evaluate and prevent the risks of thrombosis and pulmonary embolism.
Biomechanics of the Eye: Investigating the mechanics of the cornea, particularly concerning the effects of surgery or the development of biomimetic artificial corneas.
Plant Mechanobiology: Studying the mechanical and electrical responses of plants to external stresses (such as wind) and the involvement of mechanosensitive ion channels in cell membranes.
Bacterial Biomechanics: Understanding the mechanical properties and cohesion of bacterial biofilms (for example, Bacillus subtilis).

title: Open Positions
text: Join the Soft Tissue Biomechanics Group at LMS! We are always looking for motivated and talented individuals to contribute to our research in continuum mechanics, mechanobiology, and tissue engineering.

title: Open Positions
text:
Current Vacancies


There are currently no specific funded positions open.


However, we are always eager to discuss potential projects, fellowships, or future opportunities with outstanding candidates. Please see below for spontaneous applications.

title: Open Positions
text:
Spontaneous Applications
We highly encourage spontaneous applications from enthusiastic Master’s students, PhD candidates, and Postdoctoral researchers who share our passion for biomechanics.
If you are interested in joining our team, please follow these steps:




📝


1. Fill the Form
Head over to our Contact page and fill out the inquiry form to briefly introduce yourself and your research interests.






📄


2. Prepare your CV
Make sure your academic Curriculum Vitae is up to date, highlighting your relevant skills, background, and publications.






✉️


3. Email the PIs
Send an email directly to our Principal Investigators with your CV attached and a short message explaining why you’d like to join us.





Go to Contact Page

title: Chloé Techens
text:
Scholar
  
  
    
     IP Paris
  
  
    
     Linkedin
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Chloé Techens is an Assistant Professor at École Polytechnique (Institut Polytechnique de Paris), having recently transitioned from a postdoctoral fellowship in cardiovascular mechanobiology at École des Mines de Saint-Étienne (Sainbiose laboratory). She defended her PhD in Health Technologies in 2022 at the University of Bologna (Italy) in collaboration with the National Center for Spinal Disorders in Budapest (Hungary). Her academic background also includes dual Master’s degrees in Health Engineering from KTH Royal Institute of Technology (Sweden) and Mechanics of Materials from École Centrale Nantes (France).


Research Interests
Chloé Techens’ research bridges the gap between fundamental mechanics and clinical medical applications. She specializes in biomechanics and mechanobiology, focusing on how mechanical stimuli influence cellular behavior and how biological tissues behave across multiple scales.
Her main areas of research include:

Cardiovascular Mechanobiology: Investigating cell-matrix interactions and the mechanoregulation of vascular smooth muscle cells to better understand and prevent vascular diseases like aortic aneurysm dissections.
Spine Biomechanics and Orthopedics: Assessing the biomechanical consequences of minimally invasive surgical treatments (such as percutaneous cement discoplasty) for advanced intervertebral disc degeneration.
Experimental and Optical Methodologies: Utilizing advanced imaging and measurement techniques, such as 3D Traction Force Microscopy and Digital Image Correlation (DIC), often at the microscopic scale using microstructured hydrogels.
Tissue Characterization and Modeling: Designing in vitro and ex vivo models to evaluate novel biomaterials, orthopedic devices, and complex loading scenarios on biological tissues.








 Back to top

title: Simon Kouba
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Polytechnique Profile
  
  
    
     LCF Profile
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Simon Kouba is a PhD candidate jointly affiliated with the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS) at École Polytechnique and the Biophotonics group at the Laboratoire Charles Fabry (LCF, Institut d’Optique Graduate School). His research sits at the intersection of biomechanics and optical physics, focusing on the intricate mechanical properties of blood clots.


Thesis Overview
Title: Microstructure et microrhéologie de caillots sanguins (Microstructure and microrheology of blood clots)
Understanding the mechanics of thrombus formation and dissolution is crucial for tackling cardiovascular diseases, thrombosis, and embolism. Simon’s thesis investigates the mechanical behavior of blood clots at the microscopic scale, linking the architecture of the fibrin network to its overall rheological response.
By combining the biomechanics expertise of the LMS with the advanced optical tools of the LCF, his research focuses on:

Microrheology: Utilizing advanced biophotonics (such as optical tweezers or advanced microscopy) to probe the local, time-dependent viscoelastic properties of blood clots at the cellular and molecular scale.
Microstructural Characterization: Analyzing the 3D organization, density, and architecture of the fibrin network during clot formation.
Multiscale Mechanics: Bridging the gap between the microscopic interactions of the clot’s components and their macroscopic mechanical response under physiological conditions.

Key topics: Microrheology Fibrin Networks Biophotonics







 Back to top

title: Julien Bonnafé
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Julien Bonnafé is a PhD candidate in Biomechanics at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS), conducting a CIFRE thesis in partnership with EssilorLuxottica International and Inria. His research focuses on the biomechanical modeling of extraocular muscles to better understand the tissue deformations associated with eye movement and myopia development.


Thesis Overview
Title: Biomechanical modeling of extra ocular muscles for the movement of an eye model applied to optics and myopia evolution.
Julien’s project investigates the ocular tissue deformations related to eye movements and the development of myopia during childhood. By treating the eye and its surrounding muscles as a complex mechanical system, his work aims to bridge the gap between muscular contraction and long-term ocular growth.
His main research objectives include:

Extraocular Muscle Simulation: Simulating the contraction of extraocular muscles to accurately assess the resulting strains and stresses near the visual center of the eye.
Patient-Specific Modeling: Developing highly accurate, patient-specific Finite Element (FE) models derived directly from Magnetic Resonance (MR) imaging.
Myopia Evolution: Characterizing the influence of these repeated movements and mechanical stresses using advanced growth and remodeling models to predict myopia progression.

Key topics: Finite Element Method Eye Movements Growth and Remodeling Models







 Back to top

title: Thomas Lavigne
text:
Orcid
  
  
      Scholar
  
  
      Researchgate
  
  
    
     Github
  
  
    
     Personal Website
  

      

    
    
  


Thomas Lavigne is a researcher in Biomechanics and Computational Modeling, specializing in the poromechanical and multiscale modeling of soft biological tissues. His work focused on characterizing the complex time-dependent mechanical behavior of soft tissues like human skin and skeletal muscle, with direct applications in fields such as pressure ulcer prevention and tissue engineering.

Former student of École Normale Supérieure (ENS) Paris-Saclay (mechanical engineering department), He recently earned a Dual Doctoral Degree (Docteur en Sciences de l’Ingénieur) through a Co-tutelle PhD (2022-2025) entitled: Biomechanical modelling of human skin: a hierarchical porous media framework. This PhD work allied theroetical developments, numerical assessments and experimental campaigns, along with international collaboration and dissemination, with a special care on reproducibility. It successfully allowed to couple mechanics and biology (microvascular response) using porous media framework, providing insights on the time-dependent response of human skin as well as ischemic and hyperemic responses to an external load without introducing any neurological effects. This program was a joint effort between:

Université du Luxembourg
École Nationale Supérieure des Arts et Métiers (ENSAM) Paris

This work was recognized with the 2025 Excellent Thesis Award from the University of Luxembourg and proposed for the FNR Outstanding PhD Award, Prix Bézier (ENSAM), suggested for application to the FNR Outstanding PhD Award and more recently proposed for a potential participation at Prix Tarrach from Les Amis du Luxembourg

More recently, He am part of post-doctoral research projects:

Post-Doctoral Position (09/2025–11/2025): A three-month appointment at I2M Bordeaux, dedicated to multiscale modeling, HPC software install and porous fluids.
CNRS ANR Rosaly Post-Doctoral Researcher (12/2025–2027): A two-year project focused on the mechanical characterization of hydrogels intended for use in advanced cell culture systems (LMS, Polytechnique).







 Back to top

title: Odette Ngann
text:
Odette Ngann is a former intern at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS). During her one-month internship, she focused on experimental mechanobiology, specifically working on the formulation and advanced imaging of composite biomaterials.


Internship Overview
Project: Experimental formulation and imaging of collagen hydrogels with embedded magnetic nanoparticles
Odette’s work centered on the experimental synthesis and microstructural observation of smart, responsive biomaterials. By embedding magnetic nanoparticles into a biological matrix, her project aimed to explore new ways to manipulate and observe soft tissue models.
Her core contributions included:

Hydrogel Formulation: Successfully synthesizing and preparing 3D collagen hydrogels embedded with magnetic nanoparticles.
Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT): Utilizing advanced OCT bioimaging to visualize and assess the microstructural organization of the hydrogels.
Experimental Characterization: Evaluating the distribution and integration of the nanoparticles within the collagen matrix to ensure a viable composite material for future biomechanical testing.

Key topics: Collagen Hydrogels Magnetic Nanoparticles Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) Experimental Biomechanics







 Back to top

title: Siyue Peng
text:
LinkedIn
  

      

    
    
  


Siyue Peng is a motivated and detail-oriented Biomedical Engineer and a former intern at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS). Their work focuses on the intersection of medical imaging, signal processing, and machine learning, specifically applied to ocular biomechanics.


Internship Overview
Project: AI-based segmentation and reproducible workflows for ocular MRI
During the internship, Siyue played a critical role in bridging the gap between raw medical imaging data and computational biomechanical models. The project focused on extracting highly accurate geometric data of the eye and its surrounding structures from MRI scans.
Core contributions included:

AI-Based Image Segmentation: Developing advanced machine learning segmentation pipelines for ocular structures on MRI, successfully isolating complex geometries like the optic nerve and extraocular muscles using the nnInteractive framework and ITK-SNAP.
Reproducible Workflows: Building robust, automated pipelines for MRI preprocessing (including bias-field correction and normalization) and image registration.
Mesh Generation: Streamlining post-processing tasks to deliver high-quality, patient-specific 3D meshes ready for advanced clinical and biomechanical finite element analysis.

Key topics: Medical Imaging Machine Learning & AI MRI Segmentation ITK-SNAP







 Back to top

title: Gaspard Deremble
text:
LinkedIn
  

      

    
    
  


Gaspard Deremble is a student at École Polytechnique and a former intern at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS). During his 3-month internship, he focused on computational biomechanics and the development of advanced numerical tools for soft tissue modeling.


Internship Overview
Project: Development of meshing tools and FE simulations for corneal mechanics
Gaspard’s work centered on expanding the laboratory’s computational capabilities by developing a package built upon the dolfin_mech Python library. His primary focus was on modeling the complex material properties of the cornea.
His core contributions included:

Computational Tool Development: Contributing to a custom Python package based on dolfin_mech to streamline biomechanical modeling workflows.
Advanced Meshing: Utilizing Gmsh to build and adapt simplified 3D meshes specifically designed for human corneal geometry.
Finite Element (FE) Simulations: Running simulations to investigate how a gradient in material parameters across the thickness of the cornea impacts the resulting mechanical stress and strain fields.

Key topics: Finite Element Analysis Gmsh Python / dolfin_mech Corneal Mechanics







 Back to top

title: Margaux Mercier
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  

      

    
    
  


Margaux Mercier is a Master’s intern in Biomechanics at the Solid Mechanics Laboratory (LMS). Her 6-month research project focuses on the experimental characterization and imaging of corneal tissues.


Background
Margaux is an engineering student at Arts et Métiers (ENSAM) and is currently completing her Master’s degree within the Biomechanics (BME) track at Université Paris Cité. Her academic background provides her with a strong foundation in structural and mechanical engineering, which she is actively applying to the biomedical field and soft tissue mechanics.



Internship Overview
Project: Experimental characterization of human corneal mechanical behavior
During her internship, Margaux is investigating the mechanical response of the human cornea to better understand its structural integrity and deformation properties.
Her core responsibilities and methodologies include:

Experimental Testing: Performing bulge testing on human corneal tissue to characterize its mechanical behavior under pressure.
Bioimaging & DVC: Utilizing Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) coupled with 3D Digital Volume Correlation (DVC) to precisely track, reconstruct, and analyze 3D tissue deformation.
Custom Engineering: Designing custom experimental fixtures using CAD software to support physical measurements, and developing custom scripts for advanced data analysis.

Through this project, she is gaining extensive hands-on experience in soft tissue mechanics, advanced bioimaging techniques, and rigorous experimental research methodologies.
Key topics: Corneal Mechanics Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) Digital Volume Correlation (DVC)
